raw_output_payload.reasoning: We need tar archive of all subdirectories of /testbed, but without the prefix "testbed". Means we want to archive contents of /testbed/* but not include the top-level directory name? Use -C to change dir and use relative paths. For each subdirectory, we want to include its contents but not the directory name? Actually "without the prefix 'testbed'" maybe means exclude the directory name from archive path. So archive should contain subdirectories directly. Use tar -C /testbed -cf /backup1.tar .? That would include all contents including testbed? Wait . includes all files and subdirectories. But we want only subdirectories, not files directly under /testbed? "all subdirectories of the /testbed directory" implies only directories inside /testbed, not files. So we need to include each subdirectory. Use find /testbed -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -type d -print0 | tar -C /testbed -cf /backup1.tar --null -T -? But to avoid prefix, we need to use -C /testbed and then list subdirectories relative. So command: tar -C /testbed -cf /backup1.tar $(find . -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -type d) but that includes subdir names. That's fine. So final: tar -C /testbed -cf /backup1.tar $(find . -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -type d) . But we need to run from /testbed? Use -C. So: tar -C /testbed -cf /backup1.tar $(find . -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -type d) . Wait find . will output ./subdir. That's fine. I'll output: tar -C /testbed -cf /backup1.tar $(find . -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -type d)
